title: Instrumentality and Ayanami
wikitext:
[]: Instrumentality and Ayanami             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue May 18, 2010 3:39 pm Okay, we've all gone round and round with the implications of instrumentality on Shinji, Asuka, and sometimes Misato or even Gendo... but what does the whole thing really mean for Rei? I have far more questions than answers. On the one hand, it's her death; the release she's been longing for the entire series. When she changes her mind and rejects Gendo in EoE, is that because she now wants to live or because she wants to die on her own terms? Did she intend to die regardless of what Shinji chose or was that a reaction to his choice? What does it mean to her relationship-wise? Was all this her way of giving Shinji a going away present, just doing what he wanted, or was she sincerely hoping he'd choose to live in a nebulous state with her? Was reuniting with Adam part of the goal or just something she had to put up with to get where she wanted to be? Also, consider instrumentality from a sociological point of view; Rei is a dedicated Other, set apart from everyone around her by nature, design, and her own choices. Does she institute 3I to bridge this gap with everyone in part for her own reasons, to finally find acceptance with others? Is there some level of vindictiveness in this action, forcing others to see their own hypocrisies before they cease to exist? Does Rei think she is granting mercy to humanity or is she casting it aside as a failed experiment? What say you, fellow evageeks?

[]:             Quote Post by TheSophist » Tue May 18, 2010 4:14 pm does she even have a soul, isn't she basically lillith with yui's DNA?

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Tue May 18, 2010 5:41 pm She has Lilith's soul.

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Tue May 18, 2010 5:41 pm She has a soul. It started out as part of Lilith’s but because of all the time she spent with others it became truly hers in the end. Don’t forget that one of the core principals behind Eva is that each of us is shaped and comes into being by our interactions with other people, so naturally this would be the same for Rei. As for instrumentality, I think as soon as Rei merged with Lilith the process was mostly automatic. I don’t think Rei had total control over things until it got to the end and she let Shinji decided what the ultimate outcome was going to be. Up until that point I think things were moving more or less down a preconceived path. I don’t believe Rei merged with Lilith as a way to die but as a way for her to finally become her true self, the person she was always meant to be. If Rei had decided to stay as she was she would have been in effect doing what Shinji had been doing throughout the entire show, running away. By choosing to merge, she decided to face the part of herself she had been ignoring and cast aside the fake persona she was given and take hold of her true self and finally become whole.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Tue May 18, 2010 7:23 pm NemZ wrote: On the one hand, it's her death; the release she's been longing for the entire series. When she changes her mind and rejects Gendo in EoE, is that because she now wants to live or because she wants to die on her own terms? Did she intend to die regardless of what Shinji chose or was that a reaction to his choice?

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Wed May 19, 2010 9:51 am i don't believe Rei really got past the "i'm an instrument" idea. not that she'd still let herself be used at that point-- clearly, she had a lot of character development and by the time instrumentality rolls around she has bonded with people, and studied her own existence, and she knows she's not just a doll. she became aware that she did not have to be used by anyone, however it seemed to me that she also understood, perhaps then more than ever, of what she really was , that she was a vessel of lilith, and that it was inevitable that she'd have to give her life up when the time came. not because someone forced her to in order to push their agenda, but because it was her nature . like CoL said, i think instrumentality is mostly an automatic process as soon as lilith and adam are merged. basically, adam + lilith = anti-ATF and everybody turned into goo. so in that sense she didn't really do it so she could die or so she could live, she really had no say on the matter. regardless, i don't think she would've minded living on, but i believe she had made her peace with the fact that the merge was the way things had to go. it wasn't really her choice. what was her choice, though, was to put control of the outcome in Shinji's hands. maybe in trusting Shinji's decision she was putting her faith in humanity, in a way. but i always mostly saw her doing it for Shinji and not for anyone else (maybe it was the yui parallel). she didn't seem to have any expectations of the outcome, it seemed to me that whatever Shinji chose, would've been alright with her. she had grown to understand that she was a free being, that she could make her own decisions, but her decision was basically to let Shinji decide. so, basically, what CoL said.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed May 19, 2010 11:13 am Yea, yea, I know, concurrency issues, but I'll just say that at the very least, EoTV Rei no longer wants death, having come to fear it. Anyway, I figured what happened with Rei was very, very simple. She was just going to do as Gendo instructed until she thought of/heard Shinji, and then it snapped: Wait a god damn motherfucking minute! Shinji Ikari is the only one who actually gave a damn about her in a genuine manner, and not just how she'd be useful or necessary. To risk shipping fodder, he's the only person who shares any real sympathy for her at all. Fuck Commander Ikari, she's being forced into Godhood anyway, so she's going to go to the one person in her entire realm of experience that Rei thinks is deserving of all her power. Appearing to Shinji Ikari, the only person who cared for her, and so the only person she consequently cares about, she asks, "What do you want?" And she is willing to follow whatever he says.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Wed May 19, 2010 12:15 pm I like the way GreggHL put it a while back: GreggHL wrote: I think the deeper question is what does Instrumentality do, or what was it supposed to do? Because the version we're used to was either controlled by corrupt old men fearing death and desiring godhood, corrupted by a lonely man desperate to be reunited with his wife, and finally put into the hands of a thoroughly broken young boy by the girl who was so unfamiliar with the concept of love that the only way she could show it was by handing him godhood.

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Wed May 19, 2010 1:02 pm I think the main thing Rei took away from her relationship with Shinji was the ability to care. Before he showed up she didn’t care about anything or anyone, not even Gendo. Gendo had been the only one to ever care about her or show her any kindness, but she new his main concern was for his lost wife so she never really got all that close to him, never even so much as thanked him for anything as a matter of fact. Then along came Shinji. A person who got close to her and was nice to her without having any ulterior motive other then the desire to be close to another person. He showed her that life could have some value and meaning if she wanted it to. I think this reason more then anything else is why she gave him final choice in what happened. He showed her the world was a wider place then she had imagined, and so she decided to return the favor.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed May 19, 2010 1:19 pm AuraTwilight wrote: EoTV Rei no longer wants death, having come to fear it.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu May 20, 2010 2:09 pm Her concerns in ep 25 seemed to be more to do with waking up as Lilith...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri May 21, 2010 12:26 am WoT to follow: ran1 wrote: When I came to the realization that EoE primary subtextual function is to display the consequences of free will from an existential perspective, I realized that Rei's final rejection of Gendo's plan for her is one of captive/oppressor relationship... Up until this point, Rei III has been more or less had her role falsely determined by her past spectre, Rei II and of course, Gendo. Her meaning is irrevocably chosen by her when she chooses to merge with Lillith not for the sake of Gendo, but for the sake of Ikari...

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri May 21, 2010 1:00 am Good analysis, Jimbo. One other aspect to mention -- the involuntary transformation of the self, becoming someone different, can also be seen, in the context of the underlying message of both show and movies, as a metaphor for the passage through adolescence and into adulthood.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri May 21, 2010 1:19 am Thanks. You definitely make a good point that I didn't get around to. It also plays into the whole dualistic dichotomy of persona VS true self and, as an extension, individuality VS collectivism (Instrumentality). All of the characters have their "returning to Lilith" moments before EoE. Pretty much all of the mind-trip moments are an extension of this theme. If anything, the series is about how to unite all of these conflicting elements to form some kind of coherent whole.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri May 21, 2010 3:12 pm Hmm... interesting ideas so far. Rei as knowing victim of fate (though perhaps she just didn't really try to escape it?), taking Gendo down a peg on her way out the door... it kinda works. It's hard to tell just what her intentions were, or at what point she changed her mind... when the glasses got broken or when Shinji produced the uberscream? With regards to her change of feeling to now fear the end, is it existential/mortal dread as has been suggested or perhaps that she fears what the culmination of this plan will mean for those she cares about? Not afraid of death, but afraid of what she'll do, if that makes sense.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Fri May 21, 2010 4:47 pm Jimbo wrote: it's worth pointing out the limits of free-will all throughout NGE, EoE, and, particularly, how it applies to Rei (though there's a lot of parallelism with Shinji as well). Throughout the series, much of Rei's brooding is due to the fact that she senses or knows that she's destined to die, that she was born to be a sacrifice, and that she's only "herself" to a limited extent (in the respect that she's just as much a piece of Lilith).

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat May 22, 2010 12:10 am This may end up being the first omnislash debate I've had on this forum in ages. [omnislash] ran1 wrote: The so-called "limits of free will" in the series are delusions held by the characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Kutta » Sat May 22, 2010 4:28 am I'd like to insert a wee bit of lubrication here between ran and Jimbo and facilitate avoidance of philosophic meta tangent, Essentially, agreeing with Jimbo on this point, the real word characteristics of free will and related philosophy is only relevant to NGE to the extent they're presented and utilized in NGE in unaltered forms. In a way, the mere act of presenting a concept in a fictional artistic context alters it because of art's inherent "bias" of choosing what to show. However, NGE has the additional feature that it creates intricate structures out of concepts on an abstract level. What locally and on a "low level" seems to be a rather realistic depiction of personalities and events and seems to follow a solid internal logic, is utilized as a part of a highly ordered and thus highly "artificial" abstract thematic structure. To channel Jimbo, there is a dynamic scale in art that ranges from the fully character oriented mode to the point where characters are highly abstract thematic devices, e.g. in 2001: ASO where what human characters represent is infinitely more important than who they are. NGE's specialty is that it operates in both modes at the same time, creating a kind of dualistic tension. NGE does seem to use a pervasive notion of fatalism and lack of ability to bring about genuine changes throughout the show to create a thematic focus on EOE's final choice, where free will is emphasized in a cathartic way. But this is presented in an NGE-esqe subtle dualistic way rather than via blatantly established and pointed-out metaphors. Most things that happen in NGE feel like they could have happened other ways and depended greatly on choices and circumstances, and that's part why the series is believable and engaging, but on a higher thematic level they are all part of an elaborate structure. Of course if anyone would like to discuss philosophy and free will it's no issue for me at all, my attempt was only to disentangle the real world matters from how things work in NGE.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun May 23, 2010 10:06 pm ^ I imagine the mods appreciate your attempt to keep this thread from going all philosophically meta. I obviously agree, and I think it's important to be careful and not merely project one's own philosophical views onto the world of NGE. It's open enough to allow that, and certainly has enough ambiguities to allow us to fill in the cracks with whatever spackle we choose. But free will in NGE is complicated on numerous levels, not the least of which is the one you allude to about it being an artificial construct designed to be ordered and symbolic (at least, on one level) so it CAN place so much importance on a single choice.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Sun May 23, 2010 11:27 pm This is trending dangerously... Let's see more stuff like Jimbo's excellent first post and less omnislashing quote wars.

I have far more questions than answers. On the one hand, it's her death; the release she's been longing for the entire series. When she changes her mind and

I have far more questions than answers. On the one hand, it's her death; the release she's been longing for the entire series. When she changes her mind and rejects Gendo in EoE, is that because she now wants to live or because she wants to die on her own terms? Did she intend to die regardless of what Shinji chose or was that a reaction to his choice? What does it mean to her relationship-wise? Was all this her way of giving Shinji a going away present, just doing what he wanted, or was she sincerely hoping he'd choose to live in a nebulous state with her? Was reuniting with Adam part of the goal or just something she had to put up with to get where she wanted to be? Also, consider instrumentality from a sociological point of view; Rei is a dedicated Other, set apart from everyone around her by nature, design, and her own choices. Does she institute 3I to bridge this gap with everyone in part for her own reasons, to finally find acceptance with others? Is there some level of vindictiveness in this action, forcing others to see their own hypocrisies before they cease to exist? Does Rei think she is granting mercy to humanity or is she casting it aside as a failed experiment? What say you, fellow evageeks?
